name: Higgsfield AI
shortDescription: AI video platform that turns a prompt, image or product link into cinematic social-ad clips in seconds.
businessModel: Consumption-based credits plus monthly subscriptions and enterprise/team contracts. It sells primarily to social-media marketers, agencies, e-commerce brands and creators.
marketContext: Higgsfield competes in a crowded, fast-moving AI-video field. Unlike foundation-model labs (OpenAI Sora, Google Veo, Kling, Luma) and integrated model-plus-tools players (Runway), it is model-agnostic, orchestrating whichever engine is best and competing on workflow, control, distribution and price. Avatar-focused players (Synthesia, HeyGen) target a different corporate format.

valueDelivered.label: Video in minutes
valueDelivered.detail: Finished social ads in minutes, not weeks

valueDelivered.label: Always the best model
valueDelivered.detail: Auto-routes to the newest, strongest engine

valueDelivered.label: Consistent characters
valueDelivered.detail: Soul ID keeps faces and characters stable across shots

ipoReadiness: Early-stage, multiple years out. Positives: explosive growth, top-tier syndicate (Accel, Menlo, GFT), 15M+ users, a $1B run-rate target for end-2026. Cautions: only Series A, $138M raised, run-rate is a projection rather than recognized revenue, and far-better-capitalized peers.

plain: An AI video app: type a prompt or drop a product link, get a cinematic clip in seconds.
Used mostly for social-media ads and short-form content.
Customers are marketers, agencies and creators, not classic enterprise-software buyers.
technical: Not a foundation-model lab; an orchestration layer on top of other models.
Uses LLMs (GPT-4.1 and GPT-5) to plan shots, pacing and camera moves.
Routes rendering to the best third-party model (OpenAI Sora 2, Google Veo, Kling, Seedance).
Adds its own controls: 50+ camera presets, motion control, and Soul ID character consistency.
Monetizes through credits, subscriptions and enterprise contracts.
simple: Like a really good video editor that does not own a camera.
OpenAI, Google and Kuaishou built the cameras (the AI models that make the video).
Higgsfield's trick is picking the right one per shot, directing it, and stitching a polished ad in minutes.
A marketer types a few words and gets a usable ad for a few dollars, not weeks and $100,000.

differentiation.howDiffers: Higgsfield does not compete on raw model quality; it sits on top and routes to them, betting that workflow, control and distribution matter more than owning the model. The flip side: its suppliers can also become its competitors.

differentiation.theyMake: Its own video models plus a pro editing suite
differentiation.howDiffers: The most-capitalized pure-play at $5.3B and a real research lab. Higgsfield is far smaller and model-agnostic, racing on consumer virality and price rather than in-house model research.

differentiation.theyMake: A frontier video model from a Chinese social giant
differentiation.howDiffers: One of Higgsfield's rendering options, not a workflow rival. It supplies capability that Higgsfield repackages with controls and distribution.

differentiation.theyMake: AI avatar and talking-head video for corporate use
differentiation.howDiffers: They target training, sales and corporate comms. Higgsfield targets cinematic social and ad creative, a different format and a different buyer.
Well-funded model builders. Higgsfield's edge is the orchestration layer and viral distribution, not raw model research.

leadership.name: Alex Mashrabov
leadership.title: Co-founder & CEO
leadership.background: Former Head of Generative AI at Snap (built Snap Lenses); joined Snap after it acquired his startup AI Factory for $166M in 2020.

leadership.name: Yerzat Dulat
leadership.title: Co-founder & CTO
leadership.background: Open-source ML researcher and author of popular GitHub libraries; met Mashrabov via international science olympiads.

leadership.name: Mahi de Silva
leadership.title: Co-founder & Chief Strategy Officer
leadership.background: Veteran executive with leadership roles at Triller, Amplify and Verisign; earlier a senior engineer at Apple.

notes: Private company: figures are funding, valuation and traction, not market multiples. Run-rate is a company-disclosed / Sacra-estimated projection, NOT recognized revenue. Sourced to TechCrunch, Bloomberg, CNBC, Axios Pro, Sacra, PR Newswire and company filings. Brand-safety and IP controversies (Feb 2026) are live risks.

businessOverview: Higgsfield is an AI video platform: type a prompt or drop a product link, get a cinematic social-ad clip in seconds.
Founded 2023 in San Francisco by ex-Snap GenAI head Alex Mashrabov; ~$138M raised, last valued at $1.3B (Jan 2026).
It is an orchestration layer, not a model lab: it routes to Sora 2, Veo, Kling and Seedance, then adds camera controls and Soul ID consistency.
Traction is explosive: 15M+ users, ~300K paying, 4.5M generations per day, and a Sacra-estimated ~$300M run-rate (a projection, not recognized revenue).
About 85% of usage is social-media marketers; enterprise clients (Coca-Cola and Nike cited) can spend $200K+ per year.
Monetizes via credits, subscriptions and enterprise/team plans; the pitch is replacing ~$100K/min production with ~$500/min AI output.
Backers include Accel, Menlo Ventures and GFT Ventures; the CEO targets a $1B run-rate by end of 2026.
Central debate: defensibility as a layer on top of third-party models, plus brand-safety and IP controversies that surfaced in early 2026.
brief.productModel: Higgsfield monetizes a consumption-based credit system plus monthly subscriptions and enterprise/team contracts. Users generate cinematic clips from text, images or product links; the platform plans shots with GPT-4.1/GPT-5 and renders via third-party models (Sora 2, Veo, Kling, Seedance), adding proprietary camera controls and Soul ID character consistency. The wedge is professional social-media ad creative, with a pitch of replacing roughly $100K/min production with about $500/min AI output.

recentNews.headline: Higgsfield debuts 95-minute AI feature film Hell Grind around Cannes
recentNews.whyItMatters: Signals a push up-market into long-form film (a far larger TAM), but Cannes said the film was not in its official program, an early credibility risk if prestige milestones are over-marketed.

recentNews.headline: Higgsfield launches crowdsourced AI TV pilot and Original Series
recentNews.whyItMatters: An owned content and distribution play that could build a recurring IP layer beyond commodity model access; the new AI Likeness Licensing framework is an early answer to deepfake criticism.

recentNews.headline: Higgsfield ships Cinema Studio 2.0 filmmaking suite at ~$20
recentNews.whyItMatters: Aggressive low pricing on a pro suite is a land-grab against Runway, Veo and Sora and reinforces a marketer-led go-to-market, supportive of volume but pressuring gross margin given third-party compute costs.

recentNews.headline: Higgsfield X account suspended for inauthentic behavior; CEO responds
recentNews.whyItMatters: Losing its primary growth-marketing channel hits the top-of-funnel engine behind its user and run-rate growth, a real execution and brand risk.

recentNews.headline: Forbes expose details racist marketing videos and payment disputes
recentNews.whyItMatters: Reputational, brand-safety and potential right-of-publicity and false-advertising exposure that could deter enterprise and brand customers and weigh on valuation despite hypergrowth. Forbes editorial date approximate (mid-Feb 2026).

recentNews.headline: Sacra estimates Higgsfield at roughly $300M annualized run-rate
recentNews.whyItMatters: Triple-digit-million run-rate inside a year underpins the $1.3B mark (~4x to 6x run-rate), but heavy concentration in one buyer segment and reliance on viral marketing raise questions on retention and durability. Month-level date.

recentNews.headline: Higgsfield raises $80M Series A extension at $1.3B valuation
recentNews.whyItMatters: Unicorn status under two years from founding with top-tier backers (Accel, Menlo) validates the category and sets a clear valuation anchor.

recentNews.headline: Higgsfield raises $50M Series A at $1B valuation
recentNews.whyItMatters: First unicorn mark establishes the prior valuation step ($1.0B to $1.3B in about four months) and the click-to-video-for-social wedge against Runway and the foundation-model incumbents.

recentNews.headline: Higgsfield Soul image model goes viral
recentNews.whyItMatters: Soul was the viral catalyst behind the user and revenue surge, demonstrating the consumer-virality flywheel that distinguishes Higgsfield from enterprise-led video rivals.

earningsTakeaways.aiCommentary: Higgsfield's vision is an end-to-end AI video platform that abstracts away model choice. CEO Alex Mashrabov: 'We orchestrate the entire workflow. Our goal is to deliver the video and eventually deliver sales through videos.' The system plans with GPT-4.1/GPT-5 and renders with Sora 2, Veo and Kling, layered with proprietary controls and Soul ID for character consistency. He projects most social content will be AI-generated by the end of the decade.
earningsTakeaways.demandCommentary: Demand is led by enterprise marketing teams for social ad creative. Mashrabov cites customers with marketing budgets over $100 million turning 90% of their social ad creative to AI, framing the value as cutting cost from ~$100,000 to ~$500 per minute. TechCrunch reported ~$200M run-rate with 15M+ users about nine months post-launch, naming Coca-Cola and Nike among users; Sacra later estimated ~$300M by February 2026. Run-rate figures are projections, not recognized revenue.

keyRisks: Model dependency: Higgsfield orchestrates third-party models, so a supplier (OpenAI, Google, Kuaishou) can cut access or compete directly; Sora routing was already disrupted once.
Revenue quality: the ~$200M to $300M run-rate is a company and Sacra estimate and a projection, not recognized revenue (Axios Pro flagged this).
Brand-safety and legal: a Forbes expose alleged racist marketing clips and nonconsensual deepfakes, creating right-of-publicity and false-advertising exposure that could deter enterprise buyers.
Channel risk: the X account was suspended in Feb 2026 for inauthentic behavior, hitting the viral engine behind growth.
Concentration: about 85% of usage is one buyer segment (social marketers), so retention and durability are unproven.
Competition and capital: rivals are far better funded (Runway $5.3B, Luma $4B, Synthesia $4B) and several own their models.
Valuation: $1.3B on an estimated run-rate leaves little room for error if growth or retention disappoints.

comps.name: Higgsfield AI
comps.note: Subject. ~$300M est. run-rate, 15M users; layer on top of third-party models

comps.name: Runway
comps.note: Best-capitalized pure-play; builds its own models

slideBullets: Higgsfield is an AI-video orchestration layer (routes to Sora 2, Veo, Kling), not a foundation-model lab; founded 2023, $1.3B valuation (Jan 2026).
Hypergrowth: 15M+ users, ~300K paying, 4.5M generations per day; Sacra-estimated ~$300M run-rate (a projection), targeting $1B by end-2026.
Marketer-led wedge: ~85% social-media marketers; enterprise (Coca-Cola, Nike cited) at $200K+/year; ~$100K/min to ~$500/min cost pitch.
Backed by Accel, Menlo and GFT; ~$138M raised across a seed plus $50M and $80M Series A rounds.
Core debate: defensibility on top of third-party models, plus early-2026 brand-safety, IP and channel-suspension controversies.
diligenceQuestions: How much revenue is recognized versus run-rate or estimated, what are net retention and gross margin after third-party model-compute costs, and how concentrated is usage in social-media marketers?
What contractual access and pricing does Higgsfield have to the models it depends on (Sora 2, Veo, Kling), and what is the plan if a supplier restricts access or competes directly?
What is the legal and brand exposure from the deepfake and content-moderation issues, and what controls and licensing (e.g. the AI Likeness Licensing framework) are now in place?
